Logistic regression with R

Binary responses, probabilities and classification

O Rodriguez de Rivera Ortega, PhD

University of Exeter

1 Session overview

1.1 Learning outcomes

By the end of this section, students should be able to:

  • fit a logistic regression using glm(..., family = binomial);
  • explain the difference between probabilities, odds and log-odds;
  • convert predicted probabilities into classifications using a threshold;
  • evaluate a simple classifier using a confusion matrix;
  • explain why threshold choice depends on the scientific or decision context.

1.2 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

2 Binary response data

2.1 Example: penguin sex from body measurements

We will model whether a penguin is male using body measurements and species.

This is a convenient teaching example because the response is binary and the predictors are familiar, but the same framework applies to ecological questions such as:

  • species present or absent;
  • nest successful or unsuccessful;
  • disease detected or not detected;
  • habitat suitable or unsuitable.
penguins_bin <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(species, sex, body_mass_g, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm) %>%
  mutate(
    male = if_else(sex == "male", 1L, 0L),
    sex = fct_drop(sex)
  )

count(penguins_bin, species, sex)

2.2 Explore the data

ggplot(penguins_bin,
       aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_depth_mm, colour = sex)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Binary response: male or female",
    x = "Body mass (g)",
    y = "Bill depth (mm)",
    colour = "Sex"
  )

3 The logistic model

3.1 Why not use a linear model?

A linear model for a binary response can produce fitted values below 0 or above 1.

ggplot(penguins_bin, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = male)) +
  geom_jitter(height = 0.04, width = 0, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "A linear model is not ideal for binary data",
    x = "Body mass (g)",
    y = "Male: 1 = yes, 0 = no"
  )

3.2 Logistic regression

For a binary response:

\[Y_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p_i).\]

The logistic regression model uses the logit link:

\[\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{pi}.\]

The inverse-logit maps any real number back to a probability between 0 and 1:

\[p_i = \frac{\exp(\eta_i)}{1 + \exp(\eta_i)}.\]

In R, we use:

glm(y ~ x1 + x2, family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = my_data)

3.3 Fit a first model

sex_glm <- glm(
  male ~ body_mass_g + bill_depth_mm + species,
  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
  data = penguins_bin
)

summary(sex_glm)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = male ~ body_mass_g + bill_depth_mm + species, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
##     data = penguins_bin)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)      -5.435e+01  6.559e+00  -8.286  < 2e-16 ***
## body_mass_g       6.320e-03  9.209e-04   6.863 6.74e-12 ***
## bill_depth_mm     1.694e+00  2.926e-01   5.789 7.07e-09 ***
## speciesChinstrap -2.708e-01  5.058e-01  -0.535   0.5924    
## speciesGentoo    -2.895e+00  1.640e+00  -1.765   0.0776 .  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 461.61  on 332  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 161.02  on 328  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 171.02
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 7

3.4 Coefficients and odds ratios

The coefficients are on the log-odds scale. Exponentiating them gives odds ratios.

tibble(
  term = names(coef(sex_glm)),
  estimate_log_odds = coef(sex_glm),
  odds_ratio = exp(coef(sex_glm))
)

Interpretation example:

  • body_mass_g is continuous, so exp(coef) gives the multiplicative change in odds for a 1 g increase.
  • A 1 g change is very small, so it is often clearer to rescale body mass.

3.5 Rescale predictors for interpretation

penguins_bin <- penguins_bin %>%
  mutate(body_mass_100g = body_mass_g / 100)

sex_glm_100g <- glm(
  male ~ body_mass_100g + bill_depth_mm + species,
  family = binomial,
  data = penguins_bin
)

exp(coef(sex_glm_100g))
##      (Intercept)   body_mass_100g    bill_depth_mm speciesChinstrap 
##     2.490316e-24     1.881439e+00     5.440345e+00     7.627933e-01 
##    speciesGentoo 
##     5.529652e-02

Now the odds ratio for body mass refers to a 100 g increase.

4 Predicted probabilities

4.1 Prediction on the response scale

head(predict(sex_glm_100g, type = "link"))      # log-odds
##          1          2          3          4          5          6 
##  1.0266071 -0.8593696 -3.3192675  0.1468016  3.6128707 -1.2878973
head(predict(sex_glm_100g, type = "response"))  # probabilities
##          1          2          3          4          5          6 
## 0.73625758 0.29747107 0.03491608 0.53663464 0.97373420 0.21620893

Use type = "response" when you want predicted probabilities.

4.2 Plot predicted probabilities

new_penguins <- expand_grid(
  body_mass_100g = seq(min(penguins_bin$body_mass_100g),
                       max(penguins_bin$body_mass_100g),
                       length.out = 100),
  bill_depth_mm = mean(penguins_bin$bill_depth_mm),
  species = factor(levels(penguins_bin$species),
                   levels = levels(penguins_bin$species))
)

pred_link <- predict(sex_glm_100g,
                     newdata = new_penguins,
                     type = "link",
                     se.fit = TRUE)

new_penguins <- new_penguins %>%
  mutate(
    prob_male = plogis(pred_link$fit),
    lower = plogis(pred_link$fit - 1.96 * pred_link$se.fit),
    upper = plogis(pred_link$fit + 1.96 * pred_link$se.fit),
    body_mass_g = body_mass_100g * 100
  )
ggplot(new_penguins,
       aes(x = body_mass_g, y = prob_male, colour = species, fill = species)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted probability of being male",
    subtitle = "Bill depth is held at its mean value",
    x = "Body mass (g)",
    y = "Predicted probability",
    colour = "Species",
    fill = "Species"
  )

5 Classification

5.1 From probability to class

Predicted values from logistic regression are probabilities, not classes.

To classify observations, we choose a threshold. A common starting point is 0.5:

penguins_bin <- penguins_bin %>%
  mutate(
    prob_male = predict(sex_glm_100g, type = "response"),
    predicted_male = if_else(prob_male > 0.5, 1L, 0L)
  )

penguins_bin %>%
  select(species, sex, male, prob_male, predicted_male) %>%
  slice(1:8)

5.2 Confusion matrix

confusion <- table(
  predicted = factor(penguins_bin$predicted_male, levels = c(0, 1)),
  observed = factor(penguins_bin$male, levels = c(0, 1))
)

confusion
##          observed
## predicted   0   1
##         0 147  15
##         1  18 153

Rows are predicted classes; columns are observed classes.

5.3 Accuracy, sensitivity and specificity

accuracy <- sum(diag(confusion)) / sum(confusion)
sensitivity <- confusion["1", "1"] / sum(confusion[, "1"])
specificity <- confusion["0", "0"] / sum(confusion[, "0"])

tibble(
  metric = c("accuracy", "sensitivity", "specificity"),
  value = c(accuracy, sensitivity, specificity)
)

Definitions:

  • Accuracy: proportion of all observations classified correctly.
  • Sensitivity: proportion of true males classified as male.
  • Specificity: proportion of true females classified as female.

5.4 Thresholds are decisions

A threshold of 0.5 is not always best.

Use a lower threshold when false negatives are costly, for example disease detection or invasive species surveillance.

Use a higher threshold when false positives are costly, for example expensive follow-up sampling.

threshold_metrics <- function(threshold) {
  pred <- if_else(penguins_bin$prob_male > threshold, 1L, 0L)
  tab <- table(
    predicted = factor(pred, levels = c(0, 1)),
    observed = factor(penguins_bin$male, levels = c(0, 1))
  )
  tibble(
    threshold = threshold,
    accuracy = sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab),
    sensitivity = tab["1", "1"] / sum(tab[, "1"]),
    specificity = tab["0", "0"] / sum(tab[, "0"])
  )
}

map_dfr(seq(0.2, 0.8, by = 0.1), threshold_metrics)

5.5 Optional: ROC curve

The ROC curve summarises sensitivity and specificity across all possible thresholds.

library(pROC)
roc_obj <- roc(penguins_bin$male, penguins_bin$prob_male)
plot(roc_obj, print.auc = TRUE)

6 Model comparison

6.1 Add an interaction?

The effect of body mass may differ by species.

sex_glm_interaction <- glm(
  male ~ body_mass_100g * species + bill_depth_mm,
  family = binomial,
  data = penguins_bin
)

AIC(sex_glm_100g, sex_glm_interaction)

Lower AIC indicates better expected out-of-sample predictive performance, but the difference should be interpreted with the scientific question in mind.

6.2 Likelihood-ratio test for nested models

anova(sex_glm_100g, sex_glm_interaction, test = "Chisq")

This test is appropriate because the first model is nested within the interaction model.

7 Practical exercise

7.1 Exercise: build your own logistic model

Use the penguin data to answer the following questions.

  1. Fit a logistic regression for male using: # flipper_length_mm + body_mass_100g + species

  2. Compare it with sex_glm_100g using AIC().

  3. Plot predicted probabilities across flipper length.

  4. Change the classification threshold from 0.5 to 0.4.

What happens to sensitivity and specificity?

7.2 Take-home messages

  • Logistic regression models a probability.
  • Coefficients are on the log-odds scale.
  • exp(coef(model)) gives odds ratios.
  • predict(model, type = "response") gives probabilities.
  • Classification requires a threshold, and threshold choice is a decision, not a default truth.
